title: Contend for the Faith
subtitle: Warnings against false teachers, examples of judgment, and the doxology of assurance
prayer_prompt: As Jude urges you to contend earnestly for the faith once delivered, consider asking God what contending looks like in your context — and whether you've confused it with combativeness.

heb.paragraph: Kept for Jesus Christ - the recipients are guarded by divine power. Their security is not their own achievement but God preservation.
Contend for the faith once for all delivered to the saints - the faith is a fixed deposit requiring vigorous defense.

heb.gloss: to slip in secretly, sneak in
heb.paragraph: Certain people have crept in unnoticed - the false teachers infiltrated the community. Their entry was covert, their intentions hostile.

refs.note: False teachers will secretly bring in destructive heresies.
False brothers secretly brought in to spy out our freedom.
Guard the deposit entrusted to you.
Hold firm to the trustworthy word as taught.

notes.note: Jude, servant of Jesus Christ and brother of James, to those called, beloved in God the Father, and kept for Jesus Christ.
I had to write urging you to contend for the faith once delivered. Certain people have crept in, perverting grace into sensuality, denying our Master.

Calvin notes that Jude does not claim apostleship but servant status. His authority rests on his message, not his office. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: Brother of James identifies Jude as a brother of Jesus (Matthew 13:55). He does not claim this relationship but emphasizes servanthood. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

Long ago designated (palai progegrammenoi) suggests divine foreknowledge of the false teachers judgment. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

schreiner.source: Thomas Schreiner, NAC 1-2 Peter/Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Schreiner emphasizes that contending for the faith is not optional but urgent. The faith is objective truth requiring defense. Schreiner's exegesis emphasizes the eschatological dimension of the passage. The author writes with the end in view — present suffering and ethical obligation are framed by the certainty of Christ's return and the final vindication of God's people. This already-not-yet tension shapes every imperative in the text.

davids.source: Peter Davids, Pillar 2 Peter & Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Davids observes that Jude identifies himself as 'brother of James' rather than 'brother of Jesus' — a striking humility. The phrase 'kept for Jesus Christ' (tētērēmenois) uses perfect passive participle: the readers have been and continue to be guarded. This theological passive implies divine agency.

Davids notes the shift from intended topic ('our common salvation') to urgent necessity ('contend for the faith'). The verb epagōnizesthai (contend earnestly) appears only here in the NT — an intensified form suggesting athletic or military struggle. The 'faith once delivered' implies a fixed deposit of apostolic teaching.

Davids identifies these intruders as antinomians who exploit grace to justify immorality. The phrase 'secretly slipped in' (pareisedysan) suggests infiltration rather than open challenge. Their condemnation was 'written about long ago' — Jude will cite prophetic texts that anticipate exactly this pattern.

green.source: Gene Green, BECNT Jude & 2 Peter — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Green reads the opening triad — called, beloved, kept — through a social-scientific lens. In a world of competing allegiances and uncertain identity, these descriptors ground the community's self-understanding. They belong to God; they are loved by the Father; they are protected for Christ. Identity precedes ethics.

Green emphasizes the rhetorical urgency: Jude had planned a theological treatise but pastoral crisis demanded a warning. The shift models responsive leadership — shepherds must address immediate threats even when deeper teaching awaits. 'Contend for the faith' is not aggression but defense of communal boundaries.

Green notes that the false teachers' error is fundamentally social: they 'deny our only Master and Lord.' In honor-shame culture, denying one's patron is ultimate betrayal. Their 'licentiousness' (aselgeia) involves public shamelessness that brings reproach on the community. The threat is corporate, not merely individual.

hist.historical: Jude, identifying himself as 'brother of James' (and thus brother of Jesus, Mark 6:3), writes urgently against false teachers who have 'crept in unnoticed' (v. 4). These opponents pervert grace into license for immorality and 'deny our only Master and Lord, Jesus Christ.' Jude draws extensively on Jewish apocalyptic tradition, citing 1 Enoch (vv. 14-15) and the Assumption of Moses (v. 9) — texts familiar to his Jewish-Christian audience though not part of the Hebrew Bible. The letter's three Old Testament examples (Israel in the wilderness, fallen angels, Sodom and Gomorrah) and three 'woe' figures (Cain, Balaam, Korah) create a rhetorical pattern of divine judgment against apostasy. The striking parallels with 2 Peter 2 suggest literary dependence.
hist.context: Jude, servant of Jesus Christ and brother of James, writes to those called, beloved in God the Father, and kept for Jesus Christ. Though he wanted to write about common salvation, necessity compels him to address false teachers who have crept in unnoticed - people long ago designated for condemnation, ungodly persons who pervert grace into sensuality and deny our only Master and Lord, Jesus Christ.

heb.paragraph: Angels who did not stay within their own position but left their proper dwelling - rebellion against assigned order brings judgment.
Woe to them! For they walked in the way of Cain, abandoned themselves to Balaam error, perished in Korah rebellion.
Wandering stars, for whom the gloom of utter darkness has been reserved forever - they follow no fixed course, destined for blackness.

refs.note: Israel destroyed in the wilderness for unbelief.
Sons of God and daughters of men - angelic sin.
The Lord comes with ten thousands of his holy ones.

notes.note: Three examples: Israel destroyed after deliverance, angels in chains for leaving their position, Sodom destroyed for sexual immorality.
These people defile flesh, reject authority, blaspheme glorious ones. Michael did not dare pronounce judgment on Satan but said The Lord rebuke you.
Woe! Way of Cain, error of Balaam, rebellion of Korah. Hidden reefs, waterless clouds, fruitless trees, wild waves, wandering stars.
Enoch prophesied: The Lord comes with myriads to execute judgment. They are grumblers, malcontents, following their own desires, loud-mouthed boasters, flattering people.
Calvin notes the escalating examples: people, angels, cities. None escapes judgment. Privilege does not protect from consequences.
Michael restraint contrasts with the false teachers arrogance. Even an archangel defers judgment to God.
Calvin acknowledges that Enoch quotation is not from canonical Scripture but treats it as true prophecy preserved in tradition.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The angels who left their proper dwelling are likely the Watchers tradition from 1 Enoch - angels who had illicit relations with women.
Michael disputing about Moses body comes from the Assumption of Moses, a Jewish pseudepigraphal work. Jude uses it illustratively.
The Enoch quotation is from 1 Enoch 1:9. Jude quotes it as prophecy without necessarily endorsing the entire book as Scripture.

schreiner.source: Thomas Schreiner, NAC 1-2 Peter/Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Schreiner notes the rhetorical force: three examples from salvation history, three woes from Israel apostasy, seven descriptive images of corruption. Schreiner's exegesis emphasizes the eschatological dimension of the passage. The author writes with the end in view — present suffering and ethical obligation are framed by the certainty of Christ's return and the final vindication of God's people. This already-not-yet tension shapes every imperative in the text.

davids.source: Peter Davids, Pillar 2 Peter & Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Davids traces Jude's three OT examples — Israel in the wilderness, fallen angels, Sodom and Gomorrah — noting that all three appear in Second Temple Jewish literature (1 Enoch, Testament of the Twelve Patriarchs). Jude's audience knew these traditions; he invokes shared interpretive heritage to establish credibility.

Davids identifies the Michael-Satan dispute over Moses's body as drawn from the lost ending of the Assumption of Moses or related tradition. Jude's use of non-canonical material was unremarkable to his Jewish-Christian audience; the authority lies in apostolic appropriation, not original source.

Davids notes that Jude explicitly quotes 1 Enoch 1:9 as prophetic utterance: 'Enoch, the seventh from Adam, prophesied.' This is the only direct citation of 1 Enoch in the NT. Jude treats the text as authoritative for his argument without necessarily canonizing the entire book.

green.source: Gene Green, BECNT Jude & 2 Peter — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Green reads the 'dreamers' characterization through social lenses: they claim visionary authority to justify transgression. By 'defiling the flesh,' 'rejecting authority,' and 'blaspheming the glorious ones,' they invert the community's honor structure. Their rhetoric mimics prophecy while promoting chaos.

Green analyzes the Cain-Balaam-Korah triad as three patterns of destructive leadership: Cain murdered a brother, Balaam corrupted for profit, Korah challenged legitimate authority. Each name evokes a type recognizable in ancient social memory. The false teachers reenact these scripts.

Green emphasizes the six vivid metaphors: hidden reefs (danger at fellowship meals), shepherds feeding themselves (patron abuse), waterless clouds (empty promise), fruitless trees (wasted potential), wild waves (public shame), wandering stars (cosmic disorder). Each image indicts the teachers' social dysfunction.

hist.context: Jude reminds readers of three OT examples: Israel saved from Egypt but later destroyed for unbelief; angels who abandoned their position kept in chains for judgment; Sodom and Gomorrah destroyed for sexual immorality. These people similarly defile the flesh, reject authority, and blaspheme the glorious ones. Michael the archangel, disputing about Moses body, did not dare pronounce a blasphemous judgment but said The Lord rebuke you. But these people blaspheme what they do not understand. Woe to them! They walk in Cain way, rush into Balaam error, perish in Korah rebellion. They are hidden reefs at your love feasts, waterless clouds, fruitless trees twice dead, wild waves foaming up shame, wandering stars reserved for utter darkness. Enoch prophesied: The Lord comes with ten thousands of his holy ones to execute judgment on all.

sections.header: Verses 17-23 - "Remember the Apostles; Rescue the Wavering"

heb.paragraph: Scoffers following their own ungodly passions - the apostles predicted such people would come in the last time.
Worldly people, devoid of the Spirit - they operate on merely human level, lacking the Spirit who enables spiritual discernment.
Save others by snatching them out of the fire - urgent rescue of those endangered by false teaching.

refs.note: Scoffers will come in the last days, following their own sinful desires.
The natural person does not accept the things of the Spirit.
In later times some will depart from the faith.
A brand plucked from the fire.

notes.note: Remember the apostles predictions: scoffers will come following ungodly passions. These cause divisions, worldly, devoid of the Spirit.
Build yourselves up in most holy faith, pray in the Holy Spirit, keep yourselves in God love, wait for the mercy of our Lord.
Have mercy on doubters. Save others by snatching them from fire. Show mercy with fear, hating the garment stained by flesh.
Calvin notes that the apostolic predictions included warnings about internal enemies. The church was forewarned.
Building up, praying, keeping, waiting - four activities characterize those who will persevere against false teaching.
Three different responses to three different situations: mercy to doubters, urgent rescue for the endangered, cautious mercy for the contaminated.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: Devoid of the Spirit (pneuma me echontes) - the false teachers lack the Holy Spirit. Their worldliness reveals their spiritual bankruptcy.
The text-critical situation is complex with variant readings. Some manuscripts have two groups, others three. The meaning is pastoral discernment.

schreiner.source: Thomas Schreiner, NAC 1-2 Peter/Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Schreiner emphasizes the balance: build yourselves up (positive) while rescuing the endangered (outward). Believers must tend both to themselves and to others. Schreiner's exegesis emphasizes the eschatological dimension of the passage. The author writes with the end in view — present suffering and ethical obligation are framed by the certainty of Christ's return and the final vindication of God's people. This already-not-yet tension shapes every imperative in the text.

davids.source: Peter Davids, Pillar 2 Peter & Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Davids notes the rhetorical pivot: from ancient examples (vv.5-16) to apostolic prediction (vv.17-19). The 'apostles of our Lord Jesus Christ' likely refers to oral teaching rather than written texts. The prediction of 'scoffers following ungodly passions' echoes 2 Peter 3:3, suggesting shared tradition.

Davids emphasizes the fourfold command: build yourselves up, pray in the Holy Spirit, keep yourselves in God's love, wait for mercy. The structure is chiastic: outer elements (build/wait) bracket inner elements (pray/keep). Corporate spiritual formation is the antidote to individual seduction.

Davids observes textual uncertainty: manuscripts differ on whether Jude describes two or three groups requiring different responses. The core message is clear: pastoral wisdom requires discernment. Some need gentle persuasion; others need urgent intervention; still others demand cautious engagement.

green.source: Gene Green, BECNT Jude & 2 Peter — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Green notes the social function of 'remembering': shared memory constitutes community identity. By invoking apostolic prediction, Jude reframes the crisis — the false teachers are not surprising but anticipated. The community already possesses the interpretive resources to recognize and resist them.

Green reads 'building yourselves up' as communal construction (cf. 1 Cor 14:4). The community is both building site and workforce. 'Keep yourselves in God's love' is not self-salvation but cooperative effort — human responsibility within divine initiative. The community maintains its covenant position through active fidelity.

Green emphasizes the relational wisdom required: different responses for different situations. The 'garment stained by the flesh' suggests contamination risk — some rescue operations require protective distance. Pastoral care is not one-size-fits-all but contextually calibrated love.

hist.context: But remember the predictions of the apostles of our Lord Jesus Christ - they told you that in the last time there would be scoffers following their own ungodly passions. These are the people who cause divisions, worldly people, devoid of the Spirit. But you, beloved, building yourselves up in your most holy faith, praying in the Holy Spirit, keep yourselves in the love of God, waiting for the mercy of our Lord Jesus Christ that leads to eternal life. Have mercy on those who doubt; save others by snatching them from fire; to others show mercy with fear, hating even the garment stained by the flesh.

sections.header: Verses 24-25 - "To Him Who Is Able to Keep You"

heb.paragraph: To him who is able to keep you from stumbling - divine preservation is the ground of assurance. God guards his people.
Keep you from stumbling - the term denotes sure-footed stability. God enables believers to stand firm.
Majesty, dominion, and authority - the doxology ascribes to God supreme greatness, sovereign rule, and ultimate power.

refs.note: To him who is able to strengthen you... to the only wise God be glory forever.
The God of peace will sanctify you completely... He who calls you is faithful.
To him who is able to do far more abundantly than all we ask or think.
He who began a good work in you will bring it to completion.

notes.note: To him who is able to keep you from stumbling and present you blameless with great joy - to the only God our Savior through Jesus Christ be glory, majesty, dominion, and authority forever. MacArthur emphasizes the doctrinal significance of this passage within the framework of systematic theology. The text establishes principles that remain normative for the church — not merely descriptive of ancient Israel but prescriptive for how God's people should understand His character and respond in obedience.

Calvin notes that the doxology answers the letter problem: false teachers threaten; God keeps. Divine power is the antidote to human frailty. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The phrase before the presence of his glory envisions the eschatological presentation of believers to God - the culmination of salvation. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

Before all time and now and forever - the threefold time reference emphasizes God eternal sovereignty. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

schreiner.source: Thomas Schreiner, NAC 1-2 Peter/Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Schreiner emphasizes that the doxology grounds assurance in God ability, not human effort. The same God who calls also keeps. Schreiner's exegesis emphasizes the eschatological dimension of the passage. The author writes with the end in view — present suffering and ethical obligation are framed by the certainty of Christ's return and the final vindication of God's people. This already-not-yet tension shapes every imperative in the text.

davids.source: Peter Davids, Pillar 2 Peter & Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Davids analyzes the doxology's structure: God's ability to 'keep you from stumbling' (phylaxai aptaistous) answers the letter's concern with apostasy. The verb phylassō echoes the 'kept' (tētērēmenois) of v.1 — divine keeping brackets human effort. 'Present you blameless' anticipates eschatological vindication.

Davids notes the unusual fullness of the doxology: 'to the only God our Savior, through Jesus Christ our Lord, be glory, majesty, dominion, and authority, before all time and now and forever.' The fourfold attribution and threefold temporal scope exceed standard liturgical formulas. Jude ends with worship, not warning.

green.source: Gene Green, BECNT Jude & 2 Peter — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Green reads the doxology as pastoral assurance after severe warning. The community that 'keeps itself in God's love' (v.21) is also kept by God's power (v.24). Human fidelity and divine preservation are not competitive but complementary. The promise of being presented 'without blemish' (amōmous) evokes sacrificial language.

Green observes that the doxology redirects attention from false teachers to the true Lord. After cataloging the intruders' claims, Jude ascribes to God alone 'glory, majesty, dominion, authority.' The letter that began with identity ('called, beloved, kept') ends with worship. Doxology is the community's final and strongest defense.

hist.context: Now to him who is able to keep you from stumbling and to present you blameless before the presence of his glory with great joy - to the only God, our Savior, through Jesus Christ our Lord, be glory, majesty, dominion, and authority, before all time and now and forever. Amen.

rows.label: vv. 5-16
rows.text: Examples of judgment; woes; imagery of corruption

rows.label: vv. 17-23
rows.text: Apostolic warning; build up; rescue the wavering

rows.label: vv. 24-25
rows.text: Doxology: to him who is able to keep you

lit.note: Jude is a single chapter of urgent warning and magnificent assurance. False teachers threaten, but God keeps his people.

themes.note: Judgment dominates through the OT examples. Sovereignty is strong in the keeping doxology. Faith must be defended. Mercy characterizes the response to the wavering.

coaching.tip: Jude planned a different letter — 'I was very eager to write to you about our common salvation' — but changed course because of crisis: 'certain people have crept in unnoticed' (vv. 3-4). Pastoral letters often respond to immediate situations. The doctrine Jude teaches is shaped by the error he confronts.

Jude draws examples from Israel's wilderness generation (v. 5), fallen angels (v. 6), and Sodom (v. 7). Each rejected God's assigned place: Israel refused the promised land, angels left their dwelling, Sodom pursued unnatural desire. The pattern: rebellion against God's order brings judgment. The false teachers fit this trajectory.

References to 1 Enoch (v. 14) and possibly the Assumption of Moses (v. 9) are jarring — Jude quotes non-canonical texts as authoritative. This doesn't canonize those books but shows Jewish literature shaped early Christian imagination. Jude's readers knew these texts. Inspiration didn't require isolation from culture.

The doxology (vv. 24-25) — 'to him who is able to keep you from stumbling' — answers the anxiety the letter might create. Jude has warned of apostasy, false teachers, judgment. But God keeps his people. The letter ends not with fear but confidence: God's ability exceeds every threat. Pastoral warnings need pastoral comfort.
